---
title: "Lecture 25: Spring Core Basics & Dependency Injection"
subtitle: "{{< meta course.number >}} - {{< meta course.title >}}"
author: "{{< meta instructor.name >}}"
date: "March 9, 2026"
code-annotations: below
---

## {#title-slide data-menu-title="Title Slide" background="#053660"}

<div class="custom-title">
[Lecture 25]{.baby-blue-text}
</div>

<div class="custom-subtitle">
[Spring Core Basics & Dependency Injection]{.white-text}
</div>

<div class="custom-subtitle3">
Week 7 | Unit IV: Spring Framework Basics<br>
{{< meta course.number >}} - {{< meta course.title >}}<br>
{{< meta instructor.name >}}, {{< meta instructor.title >}}
</div>

---



## {data-menu-title="Learning Objectives"}

[Learning Objectives]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.incremental .body-text-m}
- Explain Spring Core motivation
- Understand dependency injection
- Compare tight coupling vs DI
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Prerequisites"}

[Prerequisites]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
- Core Java OOP concepts and interface-based design
- Basic understanding of Servlets/JSP request flow from Unit III
- Revision of Lecture 24 before moving into Spring Core Basics & Dependency Injection
:::
---

## {data-menu-title="Syllabus Mapping"}

[Syllabus Mapping]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
- Unit IV topic focus: Spring Core Basics & Dependency Injection
- CO alignment: implementation understanding + architecture reasoning
- Assessment alignment: short definitions + long implementation/design questions
:::
---

## {data-menu-title="Agenda"}

[Agenda]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
- 5-minute recap from previous lecture
- Concept deep dive: Spring Core Basics & Dependency Injection
- Code/configuration walkthrough and output analysis
- Debug checklist and exam-oriented summary
:::
---

## {data-menu-title="Introduction"}

[Introduction]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
Spring Core promotes loose coupling via dependency injection.
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Think About It"}

[Think About It]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
What changes when object creation is externalized?
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Problem Framing"}

[Why Spring Core Was Needed]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
In large Java projects without a container:

- classes create dependencies directly using `new`
- switching implementation forces code changes in business classes
- unit testing becomes difficult because real dependencies are tightly bound
- object graph wiring logic gets repeated across modules

[Spring Core solves this with IoC + DI]{.teal-text}
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Tight vs Loose"}

[Tight Coupling vs Dependency Injection]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
[**Tight Coupling (without DI)**]{.magenta-text}

```{.java}
class OrderService {
  private final PaymentGateway gateway = new UpiGateway();
}
```

- hard to replace implementation
- hard to mock in tests
- violates separation of concerns
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
[**Loose Coupling (with DI)**]{.teal-text}

```{.java}
class OrderService {
  private final PaymentGateway gateway;
  OrderService(PaymentGateway gateway){ this.gateway = gateway; }
}
```

- implementation selected externally
- easy test doubles/stubs
- cleaner architecture
:::
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="DI Types"}

[Types of Dependency Injection]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
Main DI styles in Spring:

1. **Constructor Injection** (recommended default)
2. **Setter Injection** (optional dependencies)
3. **Field Injection** (quick but less testable)
:::

```{.java}
// Constructor Injection (preferred)
public OrderService(PaymentGateway gateway) { this.gateway = gateway; }

// Setter Injection
public void setGateway(PaymentGateway gateway) { this.gateway = gateway; }
```

---

## {data-menu-title="Container Role"}

[What IoC Container Actually Does]{.slide-title}

<hr>

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
  A["Read Configuration / Component Scan"] --> B["Create Bean Definitions"]
  B --> C["Instantiate Beans"]
  C --> D["Resolve Dependencies"]
  D --> E["Inject Dependencies"]
  E --> F["Ready Application Context"]
```

::: {.body-text-m .topbr}
- container owns object creation lifecycle
- application code asks for abstractions, not concrete classes
- dependency graph is resolved once and reused
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Core Concept"}

[Dependency Injection Concept]{.slide-title}

<hr>

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  C["Client: OrderService"] -->|depends on| A["Abstraction: PaymentGateway"]
  I["Implementation: UpiGateway"] -->|wired by| S["Spring Container"]
  S --> C
```

::: {.body-text-m .topbr}
- Business class depends on interface, not concrete implementation
- Spring container resolves and injects correct dependency
- This improves testability and reduces coupling
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Configuration Styles"}

[Java Config vs Annotation Scanning]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
Spring supports multiple ways to register beans:

- **Java Configuration** using `@Configuration` + `@Bean`
- **Stereotype scanning** using `@Component`, `@Service`, `@Repository`

Both lead to the same goal: dependency graph managed by container.
:::

```{.java}
@Service
class OrderService {
  private final PaymentGateway gateway;
  OrderService(PaymentGateway gateway) { this.gateway = gateway; }
}
```

---

## {data-menu-title="Code Walkthrough"}

[Code Walkthrough]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

```{.java code-line-numbers="|1-3"}
interface PaymentGateway {}
class UpiGateway implements PaymentGateway {}
class OrderService {
  private final PaymentGateway gateway;
  OrderService(PaymentGateway gateway) { this.gateway = gateway; }
}

@Configuration
class AppConfig {
  @Bean PaymentGateway gateway() { return new UpiGateway(); }
  @Bean OrderService orderService(PaymentGateway gateway) { return new OrderService(gateway); }
}
```

::: {.body-text-m .topbr}
[Walkthrough checkpoints:]{.teal-text}

- `OrderService` never creates gateway directly
- implementation can be swapped in config only
- test setup can inject fake gateway without changing business logic
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Mini Case"}

[Mini Case Study: Payment Mode Switch]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
Festival sale scenario: enable `CardGateway` offer flow.

**Without DI**
- modify service code and redeploy risky changes

**With DI**
- switch bean wiring from `UpiGateway` to `CardGateway`
- no change in `OrderService`
- faster rollout, lower bug risk
:::

```{.java}
@Bean PaymentGateway gateway() { return new CardGateway(); }
```

---
## Memory Booster {data-menu-title="Memory Booster"}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
Syllabus memory points for **Spring Core Basics & Dependency Injection**:

- Core recall: constructor-based DI and inversion of control
- Exam compare: Tight coupling vs dependency injection
- Practical anchor: Refactor class to inject dependency via constructor
:::
---

## {data-menu-title="Live Demo"}

[Live Demo]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
**Live implementation for Spring Core Basics & Dependency Injection:**

?? **[Open Demo: Lecture 25 - Spring Core Basics & Dependency Injection](../demos/lecture-25-demo.html){target="_blank"}**

Demo checklist:
- Run same service with two implementations (`UpiGateway`, `CardGateway`)
- Show constructor injection and no `new` inside service class
- Replace dependency in config and verify behavior change without modifying service
- Demonstrate one test stub/mock injection case
- Ask students to identify where coupling exists and remove it live
:::
---

## {data-menu-title="Resources"}

[Resources & References]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
- [Spring Framework Reference](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/reference/)
- [Spring Guides](https://spring.io/guides)
- [Baeldung: Dependency Injection in Spring](https://www.baeldung.com/inversion-control-and-dependency-injection-in-spring)
- Classroom notes + lecture demo file for Lecture 25
:::
---

## {data-menu-title="Debug"}

[Structured Debug Checklist]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
1. verify the primary API usage for **Spring Core Basics & Dependency Injection** is correct (imports, method names, config)
2. check request/bean/session flow and object lifecycle assumptions
3. inspect server logs for the first exception (not just the final symptom)
4. reproduce one failing case and one passing case before finalizing fixes
:::
---

## {data-menu-title="Exam Questions"}

[Exam Preparation Questions: Short]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
- Define **Spring Core Basics & Dependency Injection** with one practical use case.
